summary: Gruber highlights Federico Viticci's examples of using Apple Intelligence with ChatGPT integration in iOS 18.2 as a practical automation tool — reformatting messy notes and extracting document structure using plain natural language commands. He draws a connection to AppleScript's original but failed promise of natural-language automation, arguing that LLMs finally deliver on that vision because they genuinely parse natural language rather than requiring brittle pseudo-English syntax. Gruber distinguishes between AI for generating original content (where he remains skeptical) and AI as an assistive automation agent (where he finds it genuinely impressive). He notes that LLM-powered automation is not only more accessible than scripting but also practical for one-off tasks that would never justify writing a script.
key_points: Viticci's examples demonstrate ChatGPT via Writing Tools reformatting messy notes and extracting document structure from natural language descriptions — tasks that previously required scripting or tedious manual work
LLMs fulfill AppleScript's original but unrealized promise of natural-language automation, because they actually parse natural language rather than enforcing brittle pseudo-English syntax
Gruber draws on his 2005 critique of AppleScript to show how its English-like facade made things harder, not easier, for both programmers and non-programmers
The accessibility advantage of LLM automation extends beyond skill level — it also makes one-off tasks worth automating, unlike scripts which are only justified for recurring tasks
Gruber maintains a clear distinction: AI as an assistive agent is remarkable and improving, while AI for generating original creative content remains suspect
money_quotes: Using ChatGPT (and the like) for automation purposes is making real the original promise of AppleScript — being able to describe automation tasks using natural language.

The best way to try to get them to do what you want is the same, whether you have a computer science degree or have never written a program in your life: describe what you want as clearly as possible in plain natural language.
Just try to ask in the most obvious way possible, and that's the most likely way that it will work, if it can work. That's remarkable.
LLMs really do just parse natural language. None of that seeming nonsense with some common prepositions working in some contexts, but other common prepositions being required in others.

Like Viticci, I remain largely skeptical and uncomfortable with AI for purposes of generating original new stuff — writing, imagery, whatever. But as an assistive agent, it's quite remarkable today and improving at a fast clip.

Not only is using Apple Intelligence for automation more accessible (in every sense) than writing a programming script or creating a Shortcut, it's also something we're all much more likely to do for a one-time task.
key_insight: LLMs finally deliver on AppleScript's broken promise of natural-language automation — not by pretending to be English, but by actually understanding it — making automation accessible and worthwhile even for one-off tasks.
